title: Device Requirements, Compatibility, Serviceability, and Asset Baselines · Evidence Workshop
summary: A guided support bench extends requirements-to-compatible-change matrix while retaining authorization, safety, baseline, privacy, accessibility, rollback, verification, documentation, and user communication. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of requirements-to-compatible-change matrix while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Separate functional, performance, capacity, interface, security, accessibility, support, environmental, cost, and lifecycle requirements.
Interpret decimal and binary capacity, frequency, transfer rate, latency, dimensions, connector markings, model identifiers, and tolerance without false precision.
Trace physical fit, electrical limits, protocol support, firmware, drivers, operating system, application, policy, and warranty as one compatibility chain.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# comptia-a-plus cumulative fictional support evidence portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic text-contract review only. They never execute learner
# commands, inspect the host, touch devices or accounts, connect to networks, open equipment,
# handle malware, erase media, contact people, or claim certification.
workspace: support
ticket-id: fictional-ticket
user-impact: [replace with user outcome, scope, severity, and urgency]
environment: [replace with asset, version, configuration, owner, dependency, and evidence time]
safety-authority: [replace with consent, hazards, data custody, privacy, access, and stop rules]
baseline: [replace with expected behavior, known-good state, conditions, and measurement]
symptom: [replace with observed behavior, reproduction, frequency, timing, and evidence]
recent-change: [replace with timeline, relevant changes, counterevidence, and unknowns]
hypothesis: [replace with falsifiable cause, competing explanation, prediction, and confidence]
test: [replace with one safe reversible discriminating test, changed case, and stop rule]
observation: [replace with actual result, provenance, rejected inference, and next decision]
repair-rollback: [replace with least-change repair, authority, backup, trigger, and restored state]
verify: [replace with user outcome, original, changed, side-effect, restart, and recurrence checks]
prevention: [replace with prevention, monitoring, owner, review date, and residual risk]
escalation: [replace with stop threshold, destination, evidence packet, and requested decision]
documentation: [replace with plain structured status, redaction, access, handoff, and uncertainty]
transfer-boundary: authorized disposable equipment or simulator only
review:

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Requirement Types
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. The workshop variation 6 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Separate functional, performance, capacity, interface, security, accessibility, support, environmental, cost, and lifecycle requirements.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Requirement Types.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for separate functional, performance, capacity, interface, security, accessibility, support, environmental, cost, and lifecycle requirements. For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Requirement Types, keep Requirement Types connected to mechanically reviewable user-impact, asset, safety, baseline, symptom, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, escalation, documentation, and transfer decision evidence.

content.text: A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. The workshop variation 6 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Separate functional, performance, capacity, interface, security, accessibility, support, environmental, cost, and lifecycle requirements.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Requirement Types condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: CompTIA A+ Core 1 220-1201 Exam Objectives — Controls every current Core 1 mobile, networking, hardware, virtualization, cloud, and troubleshooting objective and weighting.

hints: For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Requirement Types, name the user impact, fictional ticket, asset, environment, authorization, hazard, baseline, symptom, timeline, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, prevention, escalation, accessible documentation, and transfer boundary, Requirement Types precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Requirement Types, trace this concrete input and predict user impact, safety and authority, baseline, reproduction, timeline, hypothesis, bounded test, observation, rejection, repair, rollback, original and changed-case verification, prevention, escalation, and controlled transfer evidence before reviewing it: A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. The workshop variation 6 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Separate functional, performance, capacity, interface, security, accessibility, support, environmental, cost, and lifecycle requirements.

For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Requirement Types, challenge the documented misconception with changed-user, device, version, hazard, baseline, symptom, timeline, access need, hypothesis, test result, repair, restart, recurrence, escalation, and controlled equipment-transfer evidence: A vendor minimum-requirements list describes a usable and supportable system.

options.text: For commit to the mission model, apply one familiar repair, omit user impact, authorization, safety, asset and version identity, baseline, recent change, competing cause, prediction, observation, rollback, changed-case verification, accessibility, privacy, escalation, and controlled transfer, then accept a missing symptom as support proof.
State the Requirement Types preconditions, preserve earlier contracts, and name the trace or changed-input result that will decide success.
A vendor minimum-requirements list describes a usable and supportable system. That claim fails the evidence boundary for Requirement Types.

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Audit the initial evidence packet · Workshop Asset Baseline
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. The workshop variation 2 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance.

Inspect the Asset Baseline trace for this workshop case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

stimulus.title: Asset Baseline case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. The workshop variation 2 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: A hostname alone uniquely and durably identifies an asset.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance. Artifact under review: requirements-to-compatible-change matrix.

options.text: The record names the assumption, applies Asset Baseline, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.
The record says the result looks right but supplies no quantity, trace, condition, or counterexample. That claim fails the evidence boundary for Asset Baseline.

For audit the initial evidence packet, apply one familiar repair, omit user impact, authorization, safety, asset and version identity, baseline, recent change, competing cause, prediction, observation, rollback, changed-case verification, accessibility, privacy, escalation, and controlled transfer, then accept a missing symptom as support proof.

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Asset Baseline
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. The workshop variation 4 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: Create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance.

One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?

Evidence target: CompTIA A+ support Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Asset Baseline.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance. For CompTIA A+ support Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Asset Baseline, keep Asset Baseline connected to mechanically reviewable user-impact, asset, safety, baseline, symptom, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, escalation, documentation, and transfer decision evidence.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Asset Baseline symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: CompTIA A+ Core 1 220-1201 Exam Objectives — Controls every current Core 1 mobile, networking, hardware, virtualization, cloud, and troubleshooting objective and weighting.

stimulus.title: Asset Baseline failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A hostname alone uniquely and durably identifies an asset.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance.

options.text: Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim fails the evidence boundary for Asset Baseline.

For test an acceptance boundary, apply one familiar repair, omit user impact, authorization, safety, asset and version identity, baseline, recent change, competing cause, prediction, observation, rollback, changed-case verification, accessibility, privacy, escalation, and controlled transfer, then accept a missing symptom as support proof.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Asset Baseline condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Defend the transferred model · Workshop Asset Baseline
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Asset Baseline: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A low-cost upgrade physically fits but conflicts with firmware, power, application, accessibility, warranty, lifecycle, and recovery requirements. content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Create an authorized privacy-minimized asset baseline with identity, configuration, condition, ownership, support state, and evidence provenance.
